reasoning:
1. Base Rate & Initial Trend: The 3-year US Treasury Yield (DGS3) reached 3.59% on December 5, 2025, which reflects a sudden recent spike from the late-November trough of ~3.46%. Whenever a yield series experiences a swift 10-15 bp run-up, standard mean reversion implies a better-than-even chance of a short-term pullback. The explicit FRED linear + seasonal model evaluated P(exceeding 3.59) at 0.48.
2. Short-term (2025-12-14): The next FOMC meeting concludes on Dec 10, exactly within this first window. Markets are eyeing a probable interest rate cut, which generally exerts downward pressure on relatively short-duration yields like the 3-year maturity. Combined with the local peak of 3.59, a slight decline by mid-December is favored (P=0.41).
3. Medium-to-Longer Term (2026-01-06 and 2026-03-07): With expanding horizons, the variance of the yield's random walk increases significantly. Conflicting signals — the general 6-month disinflationary downward macro trend versus traders newly pricing in faster economic growth for 2026 — balance out the directional push. As forecast horizon increases, our uncertainty rises predictably, pulling probabilities steadily toward an uninformative 0.5 outcome (P=0.45 for Jan, P=0.48 for Mar).
